name: Should I repair my existing windows or replace them completely?
headline: Should I repair my existing windows or replace them completely?

mainEntity.name: Should I repair my existing windows or replace them completely?
mainEntity.text: Should I repair my existing windows or replace them completely?

acceptedAnswer.text: If your windows are drafty, hard to open, or fogging between the panes, it’s usually better to replace them than keep repairing them. New windows can improve comfort, lower energy bills, and reduce outside noise. In older homes around Pacifica, Daly City, and Half Moon Bay, original windows may have single-pane glass and worn frames that leak air and let in moisture. If you see rot, cracked glass, or feel a strong breeze around the frame, repair is often just a temporary fix. Think about cost and long-term plans. Small issues like a broken latch or torn screen can usually be repaired. But if multiple windows have problems, or you’re remodeling, replacement is often more cost-effective over time. Energy-efficient double-pane windows can help with coastal chill and wind, especially in areas like Hillsborough or San Bruno where you may heat and cool more. Watch out for common pitfalls. Don’t replace windows without checking for water damage around the opening; hidden rot can cause leaks and future repairs. Avoid choosing the cheapest window and installer without comparing warranties, glass type, and insulation value. And be careful about doing large replacements yourself if you’re not experienced—poor installation can cancel out any energy savings and lead to water intrusion and mold. Take time to get a few opinions so you’re not paying for repairs when replacement would be the smarter move.
